sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 11 Review Questions (Personality) and Ch. 12 Review Questions (Social Psychology)
sources.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)

sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 11 and Ch. 12 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Live OpenStax pages show CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

questions.stem: Personality is thought to be ________.

choices.label: D
choices.text: long term, stable and not easily changed

questions.explanation: Personality refers to long-term, stable patterns of thinking, feeling, and behaving that are not easily changed.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: This item tests a defining property of personality. Personality is generally described as what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term, stable patterns that are not easily changed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Random and unpredictable from moment to moment.

hints.rationale: Personality is a long-term, stable pattern of thinking, feeling, and behaving.

hints.prompt: Which statement is TRUE of personality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It stays relatively stable over time rather than shifting constantly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It changes completely from day to day.

hints.rationale: Relative stability over time is a hallmark of personality.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: three options wrongly call personality short-term; which one ALSO claims it is 'easily changed'?

choices.label: D
choices.text: long term, stable and not easily changed

hints.rationale: Choice A calls personality short-term AND easily changed, the opposite of the truth, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: ________ is credited with the first comprehensive theory of personality.
questions.explanation: Sigmund Freud is credited with the first comprehensive theory of personality, the psychodynamic/psychoanalytic theory.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: This item asks who created the FIRST comprehensive theory of personality. That theory is of what type?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A comprehensive psychodynamic/psychoanalytic theory of personality.

hints.rationale: The first comprehensive personality theory was the psychoanalytic one.

hints.prompt: What must be true of the correct answer to stand out from the others?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the person credited with the first comprehensive PERSONALITY theory.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is simply the oldest name listed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the shortest name listed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a currently living scientist.

hints.rationale: The answer is the originator of the first comprehensive personality theory.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which phrase in the stem is the key clue?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The blank at the start of the sentence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the item is marked easy.

hints.rationale: The first-comprehensive-personality-theory phrase is the discriminating clue.

questions.stem: The id operates on the ________ principle.
questions.explanation: In Freud's theory, the id operates on the pleasure principle, seeking immediate gratification of basic drives.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests which principle the id operates on. In Freud's theory, what does the id seek?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate satisfaction of basic urges and drives.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moral standards and feelings of guilt.

hints.rationale: The id demands immediate gratification of basic drives.

hints.prompt: How does the id differ from the reality-oriented part of the mind?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The id demands immediate gratification, ignoring reality and consequences.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The id carefully weighs reality before acting.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The id plans patiently for the future.

hints.rationale: Unlike the reality-based ego, the id ignores consequences.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: two options describe seeking immediate satisfaction, but Freud named one exact 'principle.' Which option is a plain description of the idea rather than Freud's named principle?
hints.rationale: 'Instant gratification' describes the idea but is not the exact named principle, so it is the trap.

questions.stem: The Oedipus complex occurs in the ________ stage of psychosexual development.
questions.explanation: Freud placed the Oedipus complex in the phallic stage (roughly ages 3-6) of psychosexual development.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: This item asks which psychosexual stage includes the Oedipus complex. The Oedipus complex belongs to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A specific stage of Freud's psychosexual development (around ages 3-6).

hints.rationale: The Oedipus complex is placed in a Freudian psychosexual stage.

hints.prompt: Which detail helps pin down the correct stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It occurs roughly at ages 3-6, per the explanation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It occurs in the first year of life.

hints.rationale: The roughly ages 3-6 window narrows the stage.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which is the key clue for choosing the stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the Oedipus complex defines this stage (roughly ages 3-6).

choices.label: C
choices.text: That Freud is involved in general.

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the item is medium difficulty.

hints.rationale: The Oedipus-complex-at-3-to-6 detail is the discriminating clue.

questions.stem: Self-regulation is also known as ________.
questions.explanation: Self-regulation, the ability to control impulses to meet goals, is also referred to as will power.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests another name for self-regulation. What is self-regulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ability to control impulses in order to meet goals.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The belief that one can succeed at a task.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blaming outside forces for one's outcomes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A basic bodily need for food.

hints.rationale: Self-regulation is controlling impulses to pursue goals.

hints.prompt: How does self-regulation differ from self-efficacy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Self-regulation is controlling impulses; self-efficacy is believing you can do a task.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are exactly the same thing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Self-regulation is a need for food.

hints.rationale: Impulse control (regulation) differs from task-confidence (efficacy).

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is about BELIEF in one's capability, not controlling impulses?
hints.rationale: Self-efficacy is belief in capability, not impulse control, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Jane believes that she got a bad grade on her psychology paper because her professor doesn't like her. Jane most likely has an _______ locus of control.
questions.explanation: Attributing outcomes to forces outside oneself (such as a professor's feelings) reflects an external locus of control.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests locus of control. Jane blames her grade on the professor's feelings. Attributing outcomes to outside forces reflects what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A belief that outside forces, not oneself, control outcomes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A belief that one's own effort controls outcomes.

hints.rationale: Blaming external forces reflects an external locus of control.

hints.prompt: How does an external locus differ from an internal locus of control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: External attributes outcomes to outside forces; internal attributes them to oneself.

choices.label: B
choices.text: External means blaming oneself; internal means blaming others.

hints.rationale: External blames outside forces; internal credits oneself.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is the OPPOSITE—attributing outcomes to one's OWN actions?
hints.rationale: An internal locus credits one's own actions, the opposite of Jane's pattern, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: The idea that people's ideas about themselves should match their actions is called ________.
questions.explanation: In Rogers's humanistic theory, congruence is the match between one's self-concept and actual experience/behavior.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q13 = D.

hints.prompt: This item tests a humanistic concept: the match between one's self-concept and one's actual behavior. What is that idea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A match or consistency between one's self-image and one's actions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A personality trait of being organized and disciplined.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The state of being awake and aware.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A merging or flowing together of streams.

hints.rationale: Congruence is the alignment of self-concept with actual behavior.

hints.prompt: This term is easily confused with similar-sounding words. Which describes the match between self-concept and behavior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The alignment of self-image with real experience and behavior.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being awake and aware of one's surroundings.

hints.rationale: Self-image matching behavior is the target concept.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which similar-sounding option is actually the personality trait of being organized and dependable, not the self-concept match?
hints.rationale: Conscientiousness is a personality trait, not the self-concept match, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Brianna is 18 months old. She cries frequently, is hard to soothe, and wakes frequently during the night. According to Thomas and Chess, she would be considered ________.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a slow to warm up baby

questions.explanation: Thomas and Chess classified infants who are irritable, hard to soothe, and irregular in routines as having a difficult temperament.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q15 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests Thomas and Chess's temperament types. Brianna cries often, is hard to soothe, and sleeps irregularly. This pattern fits which type?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An irritable, hard-to-soothe temperament with irregular routines.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A cautious temperament that adapts slowly but steadily.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A temperament defined only by digestive discomfort.

hints.rationale: Irritable, hard-to-soothe, irregular describes the difficult temperament.

hints.prompt: How does the 'difficult' temperament differ from an 'easy' one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Difficult infants are irritable, hard to soothe, and irregular; easy ones are calm and adaptable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Difficult infants are calm and adaptable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Difficult simply means the infant warms up slowly.

hints.rationale: Irritability and irregularity distinguish difficult from easy temperaments.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed type describes a CALM, easily soothed baby—the opposite of Brianna?

choices.label: C
choices.text: a slow to warm up baby

hints.rationale: An easy baby is calm and adaptable, the opposite of Brianna, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Temperament refers to ________.

choices.label: C
choices.text: conscientiousness, agreeableness, neuroticism, openness, and extroversion

questions.explanation: Temperament refers to inborn, genetically based individual differences in behavioral and emotional style that appear early in life.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q17 = A.

hints.prompt: This item tests the meaning of temperament. What is it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inborn, genetically based individual differences in behavioral and emotional style.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Habits fully learned later in life.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The five broad trait dimensions of personality.

hints.rationale: Temperament is inborn, genetically based differences appearing early.

hints.prompt: What most distinguishes temperament from learned personality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is inborn and appears early in life, before much learning.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is entirely taught by parents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a single trait dimension.

hints.rationale: Its innate, early-appearing nature sets temperament apart.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option names the five broad TRAIT DIMENSIONS rather than inborn temperament?

choices.label: C
choices.text: conscientiousness, agreeableness, neuroticism, openness, and extroversion

hints.rationale: Choice C lists the Big Five trait dimensions, not temperament, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: The United States is considered a ________ culture.
questions.explanation: The United States is typically characterized as an individualist culture, emphasizing personal goals and autonomy over group harmony.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q19 = B.

hints.prompt: This item classifies U.S. culture on the individualist-collectivist dimension. An individualist culture emphasizes what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Personal goals and autonomy over group harmony.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Group harmony and interdependence over personal goals.

hints.rationale: Individualist cultures prioritize personal goals and autonomy.

hints.prompt: How does an individualist culture differ from a collectivist one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Individualist cultures prioritize the individual; collectivist cultures prioritize the group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Individualist means having no culture at all.

hints.rationale: The individual-versus-group emphasis is the key contrast.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is the OPPOSITE—emphasizing group harmony over personal goals?
hints.rationale: A collectivistic culture emphasizes the group, the opposite pole, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a projective test?
questions.explanation: The MMPI is an objective self-report inventory, not a projective test. The Rorschach, TAT, and RISB all use ambiguous stimuli and are projective tests.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q21 = A.

hints.prompt: This item asks which test is NOT a projective test. What defines a projective test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses ambiguous stimuli that the person interprets.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It uses true/false items scored objectively.

hints.rationale: Projective tests present ambiguous stimuli for interpretation.

hints.prompt: To find the NON-projective test, what should you look for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The test that uses objective self-report items rather than ambiguous stimuli.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The test with the longest name.

hints.rationale: The odd one out is the objective self-report inventory.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed test clearly IS projective (it uses ambiguous inkblots), so it is NOT the exception?
hints.rationale: The Rorschach uses ambiguous inkblots, making it clearly projective and not the exception.

questions.stem: Which personality assessment employs a series of true/false questions?
questions.explanation: The MMPI is a self-report inventory composed of a large set of true/false items used to assess personality and psychopathology.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 11 Q23 = A.

hints.prompt: This item asks which assessment uses a series of true/false questions. What format is that?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An objective self-report inventory of true/false items.

hints.rationale: A true/false inventory is an objective self-report format.

hints.prompt: Which feature identifies the correct assessment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A large set of true/false items answered about oneself.

hints.rationale: True/false self-report items are the defining feature.

hints.prompt: Strategy: which clue in the stem pins down the answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The phrase 'a series of true/false questions.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: That it is a personality test.

hints.rationale: The true/false-format phrase is the discriminating clue.

questions.stem: As a field, social psychology focuses on ________ in predicting human behavior.
questions.explanation: Social psychology emphasizes how situational factors (the social context) shape and predict human behavior.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: This item tests the focus of social psychology. It emphasizes what in predicting behavior?
hints.rationale: Social psychology emphasizes the power of the situation.

hints.prompt: How does social psychology's emphasis differ from a trait or biological approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It stresses the situation over internal traits or biology.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It stresses genes above all else.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It focuses only on brain chemistry.

hints.rationale: The situational emphasis distinguishes social psychology.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option names stable PERSONALITY TRAITS specifically, a dispositional focus rather than a situational one?
hints.rationale: Personality traits are an internal, dispositional focus, so it is a trap for a situational answer.

questions.stem: Collectivistic cultures are to ________ as individualistic cultures are to ________.
questions.explanation: People in collectivistic cultures more often make situational attributions, whereas those in individualistic cultures more often make dispositional attributions.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: This analogy involves two attribution styles. What is a 'situational' attribution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Explaining behavior by the circumstances or context.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Explaining behavior by the person's stable traits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refusing to explain behavior at all.

hints.rationale: A situational attribution credits the context or circumstances.

hints.prompt: And what is a 'dispositional' attribution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Explaining behavior by the person's own traits or character.

hints.rationale: A dispositional attribution credits the person's traits.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which answer option is off-topic, pairing a bias with a bias rather than the two attribution styles?
hints.rationale: Choice D pairs two biases, not the attribution styles the analogy needs, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: A(n) ________ is a set of group expectations for appropriate thoughts and behaviors of its members.
questions.explanation: A social norm is a group's set of expectations for the appropriate thoughts and behaviors of its members.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests a concept: a group's set of expectations for appropriate thoughts and behaviors. What is that?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shared group expectations for how members should think and behave.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The specific part or position a person plays in a group.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mental script for a routine situation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An explanation offered for someone's behavior.

hints.rationale: A social norm is a group's shared behavioral expectations.

hints.prompt: How does a social norm differ from a social role?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A norm is the group's expectations for behavior; a role is the part a position plays.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A norm is one person's job; a role applies to everyone.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A norm is simply an explanation of behavior.

hints.rationale: Group-wide expectations (norm) differ from a position's part (role).

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is the PART or position a person occupies (a role), not group-wide expectations?
hints.rationale: A social role is a position's part, not the group's shared expectations, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: When it comes to buying clothes, teenagers often follow social norms; this is likely motivated by ________.
questions.explanation: Conforming to peer clothing norms is typically motivated by the desire to fit in and be accepted by the group (normative social influence).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q7 = C.

hints.prompt: This item tests why people conform to peer norms. Following a group's norm to be accepted reflects which motive?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The desire to fit in and be accepted (normative influence).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The desire to obey one's parents.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The desire to gain accurate information.

hints.rationale: Conforming to peer norms is driven by the desire to fit in.

hints.prompt: Teens copying peers' clothing are mainly seeking what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Peer acceptance and a sense of belonging.

hints.rationale: Peer acceptance is the core motive behind fitting in.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed motive is about OBEYING parents' rules, not peer acceptance?
hints.rationale: Following parents' rules is not peer-acceptance motivation, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Attitudes describe our ________ of people, objects, and ideas.
questions.explanation: Attitudes are our evaluations (positive or negative appraisals) of people, objects, and ideas.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests what attitudes are. Attitudes are our ___ of people, objects, and ideas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The way we physically treat them.

hints.rationale: Attitudes are evaluative appraisals, positive or negative.

hints.prompt: What makes an attitude an 'evaluation'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It carries a positive or negative appraisal, not just neutral facts.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is purely factual with no judgment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is entirely unrelated to feelings.

hints.rationale: The positive/negative appraisal is what makes it an evaluation.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is about how we physically TREAT people, not our evaluations of them?
hints.rationale: 'Treatment' is behavior, not an evaluation, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: In order for the central route to persuasion to be effective, the audience must be ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: The central route to persuasion works when the audience is analytical and motivated to think carefully about the content of the message.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: This item tests the central route to persuasion. It works when the audience does what with the message?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thinks carefully and critically about the content.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ignores the content and follows surface cues.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Feels happy but pays little attention.

hints.rationale: The central route relies on careful processing of the argument.

hints.prompt: How does the central route differ from the peripheral route?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The central route relies on careful thought about the argument; the peripheral route relies on surface cues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The central route relies on mood and appearances.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The central route avoids thinking altogether.

hints.rationale: Careful thought (central) versus surface cues (peripheral) is the contrast.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which answer option describes a DISTRACTED, gullible audience—conditions for the peripheral route, not the central route?
hints.rationale: 'Gullible; distracted' suits the peripheral route, not the central route, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: In the Asch experiment, participants conformed due to ________ social influence.
questions.explanation: In Asch's line-judgment studies, participants conformed mainly due to normative social influence: the desire to fit in and avoid standing out from the group.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: This item tests why participants conformed in Asch's line study. They went along mainly to do what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fit in and avoid standing out from the group.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain correct information they genuinely lacked.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Be won over by a formal argument.

hints.rationale: Asch's participants conformed to fit in (normative influence).

hints.prompt: How does normative influence differ from informational influence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normative is conforming to be accepted; informational is conforming to be correct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normative is conforming to be correct.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normative involves no group at all.

hints.rationale: Fitting in (normative) versus being right (informational) is the key difference.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed type is conforming to gain correct INFORMATION, not to fit in?
hints.rationale: Informational influence is about being correct, not fitting in, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Social loafing occurs when ________.
questions.explanation: Social loafing is most likely when individual performance cannot be evaluated and when the task is easy, so both A and B apply.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q15 = C.

hints.prompt: This item tests when social loafing occurs. What is social loafing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exerting less effort on a task when in a group than when alone.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Working harder in a group than alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refusing to work at all, ever.

hints.rationale: Social loafing is reduced individual effort within a group.

hints.prompt: Which single condition makes social loafing MORE likely?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When an individual's contribution cannot be identified or evaluated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When each person is graded individually.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When the stakes are extremely high.

hints.rationale: Unevaluated individual contributions promote loafing.

hints.prompt: Strategy: with 'both a and b' and 'none of the above' options, what must you verify before choosing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether EACH of options a and b is independently a real condition for loafing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only whether option a sounds plausible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Which option happens to be listed first.

hints.rationale: For combined-option items, check each candidate condition independently.

questions.stem: Prejudice is to ________ as discrimination is to ________.
questions.explanation: Prejudice is an attitude/feeling toward a group, while discrimination is the behavior (action) directed at members of that group.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (option A printed as 'feelings; behavior'). correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q17 = A.

hints.prompt: This analogy contrasts prejudice and discrimination. Is prejudice a feeling/attitude or an action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A feeling or attitude toward a group.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An action directed at a group.

hints.rationale: Prejudice is an attitude or feeling toward a group.

hints.prompt: What is the key difference between prejudice and discrimination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prejudice is the internal attitude; discrimination is the outward behavior.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prejudice is behavior; discrimination is a feeling.

hints.rationale: Prejudice (attitude) versus discrimination (behavior) is the distinction.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which answer option reverses the correct pairing (putting behavior with prejudice and feeling with discrimination)?
hints.rationale: Choice D reverses the pairing, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: ________ occurs when the out-group is blamed for the in-group's frustration.
questions.explanation: Scapegoating is the act of blaming an out-group for the in-group's problems or frustration.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q19 = C.

hints.prompt: This item tests scapegoating. What is it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blaming an out-group for the in-group's problems or frustration.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holding fixed negative beliefs about a group.

hints.rationale: Scapegoating blames an out-group for the in-group's frustration.

hints.prompt: How does scapegoating differ from mere stereotyping?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scapegoating blames an out-group for one's frustrations; stereotyping is fixed beliefs about a group.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Scapegoating is favoring one's own group.

hints.rationale: Blame-shifting (scapegoating) differs from fixed beliefs (stereotyping).

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed option is FAVORING one's own group (in-group bias), not blaming an out-group?
hints.rationale: In-group bias is favoring one's own group, not blaming an out-group, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: Typically, bullying from boys is to ________ as bullying from girls is to ________.
questions.explanation: Per the source, bullying by boys tends to involve physical harm, whereas bullying by girls tends to involve emotional harm.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q21 = B.

hints.prompt: Per the source, boys' and girls' bullying differ in the TYPE of harm. Which two types are contrasted?
hints.rationale: The source contrasts physical harm with emotional harm.

hints.prompt: What distinguishes physical harm from emotional harm in bullying?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physical harm involves bodily aggression; emotional harm involves hurting feelings or relationships.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Physical harm hurts feelings; emotional harm is bodily.

hints.rationale: Bodily aggression versus emotional/relational hurt is the contrast.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which answer option uses terms OTHER than the two harm types the source contrasts (introducing 'social exclusion' and 'verbal taunting')?
hints.rationale: Choice D swaps in terms the source did not use for this contrast, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: The bystander effect likely occurs due to ________.
questions.explanation: The bystander effect is largely explained by diffusion of responsibility: the more bystanders present, the less personal responsibility each feels to act.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q23 = C.

hints.prompt: This item tests the bystander effect. It is largely explained by what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diffusion of responsibility—each bystander feels less personally responsible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Becoming numb to violence over time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Simply failing to notice the emergency.

hints.rationale: The bystander effect stems from diffusion of responsibility.

hints.prompt: Why does having MORE bystanders reduce the chance any one person helps?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Responsibility is spread across the group, so each person feels less obligated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each person literally cannot see the emergency.

hints.rationale: Shared responsibility lowers each individual's felt obligation.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed explanation is about people NOT NOTICING the emergency, not the spreading of responsibility?
hints.rationale: 'Not noticing' is a different explanation from diffusion of responsibility, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: After moving to a new apartment building, research suggests that Sam will be most likely to become friends with ________.

choices.label: B
choices.text: someone who lives three floors up in the apartment building

questions.explanation: The proximity effect predicts that physical closeness (a next-door neighbor) increases the likelihood of forming a friendship.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q25 = A.

hints.prompt: This item tests the proximity effect in friendship. It predicts friendships form most easily between people who are what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Physically close to one another day to day.

hints.rationale: The proximity effect ties friendship to physical closeness.

hints.prompt: According to the proximity effect, which factor matters most for who Sam befriends?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How physically near the two people are in daily life.

hints.rationale: Everyday physical nearness drives friendship formation.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed person would Sam encounter only briefly, making them least likely by proximity?

choices.label: B
choices.text: someone who lives three floors up in the apartment building

hints.rationale: A postal worker is encountered only briefly, so proximity makes them unlikely, a trap to rule out.

questions.stem: According to the triangular theory of love, what type of love is defined by passion and intimacy but no commitment?
questions.explanation: In Sternberg's triangular theory of love, romantic love combines passion and intimacy but lacks commitment.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 12 Q27 = C.

hints.prompt: This item tests Sternberg's triangular theory of love. The love in question combines which two components?
hints.rationale: The described love pairs passion with intimacy.

hints.prompt: The described love has passion and intimacy but LACKS which component?
hints.rationale: It is missing commitment, per the stem.

hints.prompt: Spot the trap: which listed love type has ALL THREE components (passion, intimacy, AND commitment), not the two-without-commitment type?
hints.rationale: Consummate love has all three components, so it is a trap for the no-commitment type.
